docs.title: Workout Creation Tools Import Format Matrix
Status: historical / de-scoped
docs.text:
This page is preserved only as a record of an earlier manual-authored workout-package import matrix.

It is not the current default import contract for the BeatSaver/song-package direction.

docs.title: Current truth
docs.text:
The current contract after the song-package rewrite is:

  • the canonical imported package root is song-package.yaml
  • imported content defaults to song packages built around songs, charts, and sets
  • coaching media is outside the default imported song-package contract
  • package-owned environment linkage is outside the default imported song-package contract
  • future multi-song grouping should use playlist language

docs.title: Why this page was de-scoped
docs.text:
The earlier version of this page described import/storage rules for a richer workout-package lane that assumed media slots such as:

  • coaching overlay audio
  • coaching warm-up / cool-down video
  • environment background image/video
  • package-owned environment payloads
  • workout/package art as part of the same canonical package story

Those details may still matter if AeroBeat later revives a separate authored-package lane, but they are not current truth for the default imported song-package direction.

docs.title: Safe reading rule
docs.text:
If you need current implementation guidance for the default imported lane:

  • do not use this page as the source of truth
  • prefer the current content contract and tooling repos instead
  • assume coaching/environment-heavy import rules here are historical unless reintroduced in a clearly separate extension doc

docs.title: Related current-truth docs
docs.text:
• Overview
  • Content Model
  • UGC Modding
  • BeatSaver to AeroBeat Boxing v1 Conversion
  • BeatSaver to AeroBeat Flow v1 Conversion

docs.title: Historical retention note
docs.text: This page remains only so older references still resolve while the docs settle. It should not be treated as canonical for new imported-content tooling work.

docs.title: Workout Package Storage and Discovery
docs.text:
This document defines the current canonical direction for the manual-authored workout-package lane: how those richer AeroBeat workout packages are authored, stored on disk, discovered at runtime, and indexed for browsing.

Scope note: do not treat this page as the default contract for the BeatSaver-powered imported-player direction. That imported-player path is being simplified toward leaner song-package records with coaching removed by default and environment choice handled outside the package.

docs.title: Locked direction for this slice
docs.text:
• workout packages are self-contained
  • YAML is the durable authored format
  • discoverability lives in workouts.db, not in package YAML
  • the package contains the data required to validate and play the workout
  • each package has one coaches/ folder containing exactly one coach-config.yaml
  • coaching is optional all-or-nothing
  • alternate versions are created by duplication/forking, not inheritance layering
  • sets/ is the package's single source of truth for workout composition
  • workout.yaml owns package metadata plus ordered setId values
  • sets/*.yaml link songs, charts, environments, and coaching overlay ids together using ids, not paths
  • environments remain part of the package concept
  • package-local gameplay assets are not part of the official v1 package concept for this docs slice

docs.title: Durable package hierarchy
docs.text:
• Song — reusable audio and timing identity
  • Chart — one exact playable difficulty slice
  • Set — package-local composition record linking exact ids
  • Workout — the workout/package root that orders authored sets
  • Coach Config — the workout-level coaching/media registry
  • Environment — a reusable environment record authorable inside the package

docs.title: 1. Package YAML = authored truth
docs.text:
Package YAML owns:

  • songs, charts, sets, workout metadata
  • optional package-local coaching configuration
  • package-local environments
  • schema/package/tool provenance metadata

Package YAML does not own:

  • browse/search indexing
  • athlete/device calibration overrides
  • authoritative leaderboard history
  • player-wide social/profile state
  • package-local gameplay asset customization contracts for this v1 slice

docs.title: 2. workouts.db = discovery index
docs.text: workouts.db powers local browsing, filtering, moderation surfaces, and install-state lookups. It is derived data, not authored truth.

docs.title: 3. leaderboard-cache.db = disposable local cache
docs.text: The leaderboard cache remains non-authoritative, disposable, and excluded from submission payloads.

docs.title: Canonical on-disk layout
docs.text:
aerobeat-workouts/
├── workouts.db
└── workouts/
    └── <workout-id>/
        ├── workout.yaml
        ├── songs/
        │   └── <song-id>.yaml
        ├── charts/
        │   └── <chart-id>.yaml
        ├── sets/
        │   └── <set-id>.yaml
        ├── coaches/
        │   └── coach-config.yaml
        ├── environments/
        │   └── <environment-id>.yaml
        ├── media/
        │   ├── audio/
        │   ├── art/
        │   ├── coaching/
        │   └── environments/
        └── cache/
            └── leaderboard-cache.db

docs.title: Layout rules
docs.text:
1. The package root contains exactly one workout.yaml.
  2. songs/, charts/, sets/, coaches/, and environments/ are the authored content folders taught in this slice.
  3. coaches/ contains exactly one YAML file for the workout-level coach config domain.
  4. Media/resource references should resolve inside the package folder.
  5. cache/ contains only disposable derived data.

docs.title: Set composition rule
docs.text:
A Set links:

  • one songId
  • one chartId
  • one environmentId
  • optional coachingOverlayId

The older assetSelections package pattern is intentionally removed from the official workout-package contract in this docs pass.

docs.title: Environment direction
docs.text:
Environment records remain part of the package concept because they meaningfully shape the workout experience without reopening the older package-asset sprawl.

The locked Environment v1 type enum remains:

  • image_background
  • video_background
  • glb_environment
  • splat

Baseline godot_scene should still be treated as an advanced future controlled-pipeline path, not a default loose-package handoff. splat is now an official package-facing environment type, but it remains a controlled advanced path: AeroBeat should prefer .compressed.ply as the official recommended package payload, while .ply, .splat, and .sog remain compatibility-supported through GDGS. The exported workout still copies the chosen splat payload into media/environments/ so the package stays self-contained, and the currently validated runtime path is desktop / Forward Plus / compute-bound rather than a broad cross-device guarantee.

docs.title: Coaching direction
docs.text:
Coaching stays inside the package's single coaches/coach-config.yaml domain.

  • disabled coaching may be represented by enabled: false
  • enabled coaching owns the roster, warmup video, cooldown video, and overlay audio registry
  • sets reference enabled overlay clips through coachingOverlayId

docs.title: Validation expectations
docs.text:
Tooling for this slice should validate:

  • the authored YAML records under workout.yaml, songs/, charts/, sets/, coaches/, and environments/
  • the checked-in SQL schema files under sql/
  • package-local id integrity across workout/set/song/chart/environment/coaching references

docs.title: Product note on customization
docs.text: Removing package-local gameplay assets from the workout-package contract does not mean AeroBeat is abandoning customization. It means customization should move toward a more controlled product path such as profile-driven avatar and cosmetics unlocks tied to workout progression.

docs.title: AeroBeat: Concept Overview
docs.text:
AeroBeat is an open-source free-to-play rhythm workout platform with a deliberately narrowed v1: camera-based Boxing and Flow, shipped to the PC community first.

Older docs described AeroBeat as broadly gameplay-agnostic, hardware-agnostic, and ready to launch across many devices at once. That is no longer the official v1 product claim. The current product thesis is narrower on purpose so the team can ship a strong, coherent first experience.

docs.title: High concept
docs.text:
Accessible free-to-play rhythm workouts through a standard camera, with BeatSaver-powered song play on PC.

AeroBeat still keeps a modular architecture, but the v1 product promise is now specific:

  1. Business model: free-to-play app with a free core experience
  2. Input: official gameplay support is camera tracking
  3. Gameplay: official v1 features are Boxing and Flow
  4. Content: imported song packages with multiple converted charts/difficulties under one song root
  5. Grouping: future multi-song sessions should be described as playlists
  6. Progression: AeroBeat-owned profile, workout points, and controlled avatar/cosmetics customization

docs.title: Free-to-play without identity drift
docs.text:
AeroBeat is not just a free download. It is a free-to-play product that needs a clear long-term answer for athlete identity, progression, and workout access.

That means the architecture cannot quietly collapse into vendor-owned identity just because third-party catalog/community services exist. AeroBeat still needs an AeroBeat-owned account architecture as a first-class design concern, even if the full end-user account product is phased.

docs.title: Accessibility first through camera play
docs.text: The first launch should let athletes start with hardware they already own: a laptop or desktop plus a camera. UI navigation may still use mouse on PC and touch on mobile surfaces, but official gameplay support is camera-first.

docs.title: Fitness-first choreography
docs.text: Boxing and Flow are the retained gameplay pillars because they align with the current workout thesis: readable full-body movement, cardio, rhythm, and progression without requiring a controller ecosystem.

docs.title: Community-first release order
docs.text:
The release priority is:

  1. PC community first
  2. mobile second
  3. VR third

That order should shape planning language throughout the docs. Mobile and VR remain meaningful future targets, but they are not current parity promises.

docs.title: Song packages first, playlists later
docs.text:
The default content model should support:

  • one imported song package per source song/root
  • multiple exact charts/difficulties under that same package
  • future playlists for grouping many song packages together

The default imported-player path should not drag along manual-authored workout-package baggage such as required package-local coaching or required package-owned environments.

docs.title: Modular architecture, scoped promises
docs.text: AeroBeat still benefits from modular content, input, and feature boundaries. The key change is product truthfulness: future-compatible architecture does not mean every feature, device, or platform is official v1 scope.

docs.title: Explicitly out of current gameplay scope
docs.text:
• Dance as a gameplay feature
  • Step as a gameplay feature
  • official non-camera gameplay input support
  • portal-era authored chart semantics as default truth
  • package-local gameplay asset swapping as a core content concept
  • manual-authored one-difficulty workout packages as the default imported-player story

docs.title: Still valid but future-facing
docs.text:
• JoyCon, gamepad, keyboard, touch, mouse, and XR input providers
  • VR-focused presentation and shells
  • broader platform packaging after the PC-first community release
  • optional future curated/manual-authored content flows beyond imported song packages

docs.title: Content direction
docs.text:
Default imported content should focus on the durable records that matter most for this slice:

  • song packages
  • songs
  • charts
  • sets
  • playlists

Older package-heavy coaching/environment examples are being retired from the default truth surfaces. Future customization docs should point toward controlled avatar and cosmetics unlocks via workout points rather than teaching freeform package-local gameplay asset swaps as the main product path.

docs.title: Art Inspiration
docs.text:
• Direction: natural wonder with digital zen UI
  • Aesthetic: premium fitness with clean, high-contrast readability
  • Constraint: visuals should serve the PC-first camera workout experience first, while staying portable to later mobile and VR work where possible
  • Nature Inspired: choose environments inspired by strong outdoor or studio energy

docs.title: Art domains that still matter
docs.text:
• Environments for workout atmosphere
  • Avatars for player/coach identity
  • Cosmetics for controlled profile customization
  • Core internal assets for UI, branding, and runtime presentation

docs.title: Scope note
docs.text: Older docs emphasized package-local gameplay skins/assets as a creator-facing product pillar. The current docs slice shifts customization toward controlled avatar/cosmetics progression instead.

docs.title: Workout Points (WP) Economy
docs.text: This document details the "Workout Points" (WP) economy, the primary currency in AeroBeat used to unlock cosmetic items.

docs.title: 1. Philosophy
docs.text: The goal of the WP economy is to incentivize consistency. We reward effort (Time + Difficulty) rather than just skill.

docs.title: 2. Earning WP (The Faucet)
docs.text: WP is calculated at the end of every completed Workout.

docs.title: The Formula
docs.text: WP_Earned = (Base_Rate * Duration_Mins * Difficulty_Mult) + Accuracy_Bonus

docs.title: Variables
docs.text:
• Base Rate: 10 WP per minute of active gameplay.
  • Duration: The total length of the completed Workout in minutes (rounded down).
  • Difficulty Multiplier:
    • Easy: 1.0x
    • Normal: 1.2x
    • Hard: 1.5x
    • Pro: 2.0x
  • Accuracy Bonus: +50 WP Flat Bonus if Session Accuracy > 90%.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: Scenario Duration Difficulty Accuracy Calculation Total WP Quick Warmup 10 mins Easy (1.0x) 85% (10 * 10 * 1.0) + 0 100 WP Standard Workout 30 mins Normal (1.2x) 92% (10 * 30 * 1.2) + 50 410 WP Intense Session 45 mins Hard (1.5x) 95% (10 * 45 * 1.5) + 50 725 WP

docs.title: 3. Spending WP (The Sink)
docs.text:
WP is spent in the Locker Room to unlock official cosmetic items for the Avatar.

V1 scope note:

  • WP should ship as the single launch progression currency
  • AeroBeat does not need a broad live-ops shop, premium-currency exchange, or battle-pass economy in v1
  • a small curated set of official unlocks is enough for launch if content supply is ready
  • larger sinks such as weekly rotations or consumable boosters are future options, not launch requirements

docs.title: Pricing Tiers
docs.text: Tier Cost Est. Workout Time Example Items Common 500 WP ~30 mins Basic colors, Wristbands. Uncommon 2,500 WP ~3 hours Patterned textures, Sunglasses. Rare 10,000 WP ~2 weeks Glowing materials, Neon gloves. Legendary 50,000 WP ~2 months Particle effect auras, Complex models.

docs.title: 4. Risk Analysis & Mitigation
docs.text: Risk Impact Mitigation Strategy Local Save Editing Users edit user_profile.tres to give themselves infinite WP. Obfuscation. Switch save format from text-based .tres to binary .res or encrypted JSON for the profile. While not hack-proof, it deters casual editing. Economy Inflation Hardcore players accumulate millions of WP with nothing to buy. Consumable Sinks. Introduce "Workout Boosters" (e.g., "Double XP for 30 mins") or "Visual Effects" (e.g., "Fire Trail for 1 song") that cost WP to use. Content Drought Players unlock all cosmetics too quickly. Weekly Rotations. Implement a "Featured Shop" logic in the client that only exposes a subset of items for purchase each week, even if the assets are local. UGC Conflicts A modder releases a "Free" version of a "Legendary" paid skin. ID Namespacing. Official items use aerobeat.official.*. UGC uses aerobeat.ugc.*. The Shop only lists Official items. UGC is always free/unlocked but separated in the UI. "Pay-to-Win" Cosmetics obscure vision or change hitboxes. Strict Validation. The aerobeat-cosmetics template must enforce bounding box limits. The game engine will force-unload cosmetics that exceed polycount or volume limits during gameplay.

docs.title: Supporter Perks & Benefits
docs.text: AeroBeat operates on a "Support, Don't Buy" model. The core game is free, and becoming a Supporter is a voluntary contribution to help fund servers and development. In return, Supporters receive a suite of account-level perks focused on convenience, customization, and community status.

docs.title: 1. Pricing
docs.text:
Supporter tags are purchased as non-recurring products (time blocks).

  • 1 Month: $4.00
  • 2 Months: $8.00
  • 4 Months: $12.00 (Save 25%)
  • 6 Months: $16.00 (Save 33%)
  • 1 Year: $24.00 (Save 50%)
  • 18 Months: $39.00 (Save 46%)
  • 2 Years: $52.00 (Save 46%)

docs.title: A. Social Status (The "Clout")
docs.text:
Stand out in the community.

  • In-Game Badge: A unique "Supporter Heart" icon displayed next to your username on Leaderboards, Lobbies, and Profile.
  • Discord Role: Automatically syncs with the official Discord server to grant the "AeroBeat Supporter" role, unlocking private channels.
  • Profile Flair: Gold border and special visual effects on your Web Profile and In-Game Badge.

docs.title: B. Convenience & Limits
docs.text:
Power-user features for heavy users.

  • Cloud Storage: Increased quota for UGC uploads (5GB vs 2GB for free users).
  • Friends List: Cap increased from 500 to 1000 friends.
  • Download Priority: Higher bandwidth limits for downloading mods from the CDN during peak traffic times.

docs.title: C. Cosmetic Exclusives
docs.text:
Exclusive customization options.

  • Locker Room Access: Unlocks visibility of the "Supporter Collection" in the Avatar Shop.
    • Note: Items still require Workout Points (WP) to purchase.
  • Menu Customization: Ability to change the Main Menu background environment to any downloaded Environment mod (Free users are locked to the default).

docs.title: D. Community Influence
docs.text:
Help shape the future of AeroBeat.

  • Weighted Voting: Supporter votes count double on "Feature Roadmap" polls.
  • Workout Nominations: Ability to nominate workouts for the "Workout of the Day" featured slot.

docs.title: E. Multiplayer Enhancements
docs.text:
Express yourself in online lobbies.

  • Lobby Host Customization: When hosting a lobby, Supporters can change the visual environment (Skybox/Lighting) for all players in the waiting room.
  • Entrance FX: Unique particle effects (e.g., "Lightning Strike", "Digital Materialize") when joining a session.
  • Lobby Emotes: Access to exclusive animated stickers and reactions in multiplayer chat.

docs.title: F. Advanced Analytics (The "Pro" Stats)
docs.text:
Deep dive into your performance data.

  • Extended History: View your entire workout history (Free users are limited to the last 10 sessions).
  • Performance Heatmaps: Visual breakdown of accuracy per lane/sector to identify weak spots (e.g., "You consistently miss low-left targets").

docs.title: G. Interface Customization
docs.text:
Tailor the HUD to your preference.

  • HUD Skins: Ability to theme the in-game scoring UI (Combo Counter, Health Bar) with different styles (e.g., "Retro", "Minimalist").

docs.title: H. External Integrations (The "Connected Athlete")
docs.text:
Sync your data with your fitness ecosystem.

  • Health Platform Sync: Automatic synchronization of workout data (Calories, Duration, Heart Rate) to external platforms like Strava, Apple Health, or Google Fit.
  • Data Export: Ability to download raw CSV logs of your workout history for personal analysis.

docs.title: I. Social Structures (Crews)
docs.text:
Build your own community.

  • Crew Creation: Ability to found and manage a "Crew" (Guild/Clan). Includes a custom Tag (e.g., [NEON]), a private leaderboard, and management tools.
    • Note: Free players can join Crews, but only Supporters can create them.

docs.title: 3. Philosophy on Restrictions
docs.text:
We strictly adhere to a Free-to-Win policy.

  • No Gameplay Locking: We never lock Songs, Modes, or Maps behind the Supporter tag.
  • No Pay-to-Win: Supporters do not get gameplay advantages (e.g., higher scores or easier hit windows).

docs.title: Gameplay Core: Boxing
docs.text: Boxing is one of the two official AeroBeat v1 gameplay features.

docs.title: Core loop
docs.text:
1. Boxing beats arrive in time with the music.
  2. The athlete throws timed straights, hooks, uppercuts, guards, squats, and weaves as authored by the chart.
  3. Successful reads maintain combo and reinforce workout flow.
  4. The workout emphasizes cardio, rhythm, and readable movement patterns.

docs.title: Scope notes for this slice
docs.text:
• official gameplay input is camera only
  • Boxing should be documented primarily around camera-readable movement
  • the current chart contract is semantic Boxing gameplay, not portal-driven authored placement

docs.title: Current mechanics emphasis
docs.text:
• handed straight_*, hook_*, and uppercut_* strikes
  • guard, squat, and weave_* movement prompts
  • readable Track View and Portal View presentation options without making portal semantics part of the chart contract

docs.title: BeatSaver conversion reference
docs.text:
For the canonical BeatSaver-to-Boxing v1 conversion rules, ignored objects, and unresolved source edge cases, see:

  • BeatSaver to AeroBeat Boxing v1 Conversion

This gameplay page stays focused on the feature itself; the conversion contract lives in Architecture.

docs.title: Wording guardrails
docs.text:
When describing Boxing, prefer language that supports the current product thesis:

  • camera-first gameplay
  • PC-first release
  • semantic Boxing chart vocabulary aligned to straight_*, hook_*, uppercut_*, guard, squat, and weave_*
  • future VR/non-camera possibilities without presenting them as current parity or contract truth

docs.title: Gameplay Core: Flow
docs.text: Flow is the second official AeroBeat v1 gameplay feature.

docs.title: Core loop
docs.text:
1. Flow gameplay uses a calibrated athlete-relative 4x3 play grid.
  2. The athlete enters target cells with the correct wrist in time with the music.
  3. Arrow-note correctness comes from recent wrist motion direction; dot-note correctness comes from timed cell entry without an added direction requirement.
  4. Successful reads maintain combo and support a smooth full-body workout rhythm.

docs.title: Scope notes for this slice
docs.text:
• official gameplay input is camera only
  • Flow remains an active retained feature
  • the old portal/placement authored model is not current product truth
  • broader device/input permutations can stay future-facing, not official v1 parity claims

docs.title: Mechanics emphasis
docs.text:
• direct 4x3 cell-entry gameplay
  • rolling wrist-motion direction checks for arrow notes
  • bombs as wrist-space hazards to avoid
  • obstacles as nose-space occupancy bans
  • arcs/sliders as guidance-only visuals in v1
  • movement that remains understandable on the PC-first camera product path

docs.title: BeatSaver conversion reference
docs.text:
For the canonical BeatSaver-to-Flow v1 conversion rules, object treatment, frozen burst schema, and still-open implementation seams, see:

  • BeatSaver to AeroBeat Flow v1 Conversion

This gameplay page stays focused on the feature itself; the conversion contract lives in Architecture.

docs.title: View Modes & Optimizations
docs.text: AeroBeat still separates gameplay logic from runtime presentation, but current product wording should favor the PC-first camera slice.

docs.title: Portal View
docs.text:
• immersive 3D presentation
  • still useful as a runtime/view concept
  • relevant for future VR return and larger-screen experiences

docs.title: Track View
docs.text:
• linear 2D presentation family
  • especially important for compact or straightforward PC camera-friendly layouts

docs.title: Scope guidance
docs.text:
The docs should avoid implying that VR and mobile view parity are already equal-status shipping targets.

  • PC camera presentation is the main current priority
  • mobile-oriented adaptation remains follow-on work
  • VR-oriented presentation remains a later platform slice

docs.title: Runtime adaptation
docs.text: Renderer adaptation across views still matters, especially for preserving authored choreography across different presentation surfaces. That is a technical capability, not a promise of immediate platform parity.

docs.title: Gamification & Habit Building
docs.text:
AeroBeat should reward consistency first, not endless meta-system complexity.

For the launch product, the retention loop should stay narrow and honest:

  • Workout Points (WP) as the single launch progression currency
  • simple weekly goals
  • simple streaks tied to consistency

That is enough to make progress visible without turning v1 into a premature live-service treadmill.

See also:

  • Account and Retention Feature Phasing
  • V1 Account and Retention Behavior

docs.title: Workout Points
docs.text:
Workout Points remain the main progression currency in v1.

Their job is simple:

  • reward completed workouts
  • make progress visible
  • support a modest set of official unlocks if content supply is ready

WP should not imply a large rotating store, premium-currency exchange, or battle-pass system at launch.

docs.title: Weekly goals
docs.text:
AeroBeat should let athletes pursue a simple weekly consistency target.

Recommended v1 examples:

  • workout X days this week
  • complete X sessions this week

The rule should be easy to understand and aligned with real workout habits.

docs.title: Streaks
docs.text:
Streaks should reinforce habit-building, not punish normal life.

Recommended v1 posture:

  • prefer a weekly consistency streak over a brittle daily-login streak
  • avoid paid streak repair or similar manipulative mechanics

docs.title: Deliberately deferred from v1
docs.text:
These may be valuable later, but they should not define the launch retention loop:

  • global leaderboards
  • social accountability graphs and crews
  • premium currency
  • battle passes
  • seasonal progression tracks
  • rotating shops

Those systems should be added only after the base workout loop, entitlement flows, and simple progression loop are proven.

docs.title: Explorer bonus
docs.text: Variety goals in the current docs slice should encourage athletes to explore the retained gameplay features, especially Boxing and Flow, rather than referencing removed modes.

docs.title: Daily Quests System
docs.text: Daily quests provide short-term goals that reinforce consistency and progression.

docs.title: Example quest directions for this slice
docs.text:
• play songs in Boxing mode
  • play songs in Flow mode
  • complete workouts
  • hit time, accuracy, or consistency goals

Quest examples should stay aligned with the active gameplay set rather than referencing removed modes.

docs.title: Project Glossary
docs.text:
Use this glossary to keep the docs set aligned with the current AeroBeat scope.

Term Definition Song Package A self-contained imported song-root folder containing package metadata plus the song/charts/sets needed for local playback. It may contain multiple exact playable difficulties or feature slices for the same imported source song. Song The reusable audio and timing source for gameplay content. Chart One concrete playable authored or converted chart representing a single feature+difficulty slice. Set The exact playable record that links one Song and one Chart. Playlist A future multi-song grouping above song packages. Input Provider The runtime abstraction that bridges hardware input systems to gameplay logic. Input Profile A concrete runtime or device compatibility target, such as mediapipe_camera or another future provider profile. Track View The broader family of linear 2D runtime presentation modes. Portal View A presentation/view term only. It is not current authored chart-contract truth. Avatar A 3D character model representing the player or coach. Cosmetic An account-level accessory or presentation unlock for an avatar or profile identity. leaderboard-cache.db The local disposable per-song-package SQLite cache used for leaderboard browsing where applicable.

docs.title: Input Strategy
docs.text: AeroBeat still uses a normalized input architecture, but the product messaging is now more specific than the older "input agnostic" framing.

docs.title: Official v1 gameplay input
docs.text:
Camera is the only official AeroBeat v1 gameplay input.

That applies to the current Boxing and Flow product slice. The game should be documented, validated, and marketed around camera-driven gameplay first.

docs.title: Why the architecture still stays modular
docs.text:
Keeping an input-provider architecture is still useful because it lets the platform:

  • isolate camera implementation details from gameplay logic
  • keep room for future accessibility and platform work
  • preserve future paths for mobile-native camera, XR, JoyCon, keyboard, gamepad, mouse, and touch experiments

That architectural flexibility should not be mistaken for current shipping parity.

docs.title: Tier 1: Official gameplay support
docs.text:
• Camera on PC via MediaPipe or successor camera tracking stacks
  • Camera on mobile remains a planned follow-on path, not the first release priority

docs.title: Tier 2: Future-input support worth preserving in docs
docs.text:
These inputs may remain documented at the repo/API level because they are useful future work:

  • JoyCon
  • keyboard
  • gamepad
  • mouse
  • touch
  • XR

For now, treat them as future support, testing hooks, accessibility experiments, or platform exploration rather than official v1 gameplay promises.

docs.title: Navigation vs gameplay
docs.text:
AeroBeat now draws a clearer line between menu navigation and gameplay input:

  • Gameplay: camera only for official v1
  • UI navigation on PC: mouse is fine
  • UI navigation on mobile: touch is fine

That distinction lets the docs stay honest without pretending every pointer or controller path is a first-class workout experience today.

docs.title: The Locker Room (Customization & Shop)
docs.text: The Locker Room is the immersive 3D space where athletes customize their Avatar. It functions as both an Inventory Manager and the Item Shop.

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text:
• Role: Avatar customization and spending "Workout Points" (WP).
  • Perspective: 3D "Fitting Room" view.
  • Requirement: Online connection required for transactions (Server Authority).

docs.title: 2. Layout & UX
docs.text: The screen is divided into three zones to maximize the view of the Avatar.

docs.title: A. The Stage (Center)
docs.text:
• Content: The athlete's 3D Avatar.
  • Controls:
    • Rotate: Drag horizontally.
    • Zoom: Pinch / Scroll Wheel.
    • Reset: Button to return to default view.

docs.title: B. The Category Rack (Left Panel)
docs.text:
Navigation for different body slots.

  • Slots:
    • Head (Hats, Helmets)
    • Face (Glasses, Masks)
    • Torso (Shirts, Jackets)
    • Hands (Gloves, Watches)
    • Legs (Pants, Shorts)
    • Feet (Shoes)
    • Accessories (Backpacks, Auras)

docs.title: C. The Shelf (Right Panel)
docs.text:
A grid displaying items for the selected category.

  • Tabs: "Owned" (Inventory) vs "Shop" (Unowned).
  • Item Card:
    • Thumbnail: Icon of the item.
    • Status: Equipped / Owned / Price (WP).
    • Rarity: Border color (Common/Rare/Legendary).
    • Exclusivity: Supporter Icon (if applicable).

docs.title: 3. The "Fitting Room" Logic
docs.text: We allow players to try before they buy.

docs.title: Preview Mode
docs.text:
1. Clicking an Unowned item temporarily equips it on the Avatar.
  2. The "Buy" button appears at the bottom of the screen.
  3. Leaving the category or exiting the Locker Room reverts the Avatar to its previous state unless purchased.

docs.title: Conflict Resolution
docs.text:
Some items cannot be worn together (e.g., a Full Helmet and Sunglasses).

  • Logic: If a player equips an item that conflicts with a currently equipped item, the conflicting item is automatically unequipped.
  • Feedback: A small toast notification appears: "Unequipped 'Aviator Shades' due to conflict."

docs.title: Runtime Gizmos (The "Perfect Fit")
docs.text:
Since UGC Avatars vary in shape, accessories might clip.

  • Feature: When an accessory is selected, an "Adjust" button appears.
  • Gizmo: Allows minor Position, Rotation, and Scale adjustments relative to the attachment bone.
  • Persistence: These offsets are saved in the AeroUserProfile for that specific (AvatarID, ItemID) pair.

docs.title: Purchasing Flow
docs.text:
To prevent accidental spending, we use a "Hold to Confirm" interaction.

  1. Select: Player clicks an unowned item (e.g., "Neon Visor - 500 WP").
  2. Preview: Avatar wears the item.
  3. Buy: The "Purchase" button becomes active.
    • State: If Wallet < Cost, button is Disabled (Red).
    • State: If Wallet >= Cost, button is Enabled (Green).
  4. Confirm: Player holds the button for 1.0s. A radial fill animation plays.
  5. Transaction:
    • Client sends POST /api/store/purchase to Server.
    • Server validates funds, deducts WP, adds Item ID to inventory.
    • Server responds 200 OK.
  6. Unlock: UI plays "Unlock FX", deducts WP from HUD, and switches item state to "Owned".

docs.title: Supporter Exclusives
docs.text:
Certain cosmetic items are tagged as Supporter Exclusive.

  • Visibility: These items are hidden from the Shop for free players (or shown with a lock icon).
  • Purchase: Only active Supporters can purchase them.
  • Cost: They still require Workout Points (WP) to unlock. Being a Supporter grants access, not the item itself.

docs.title: Online Requirement
docs.text:
• Validation: The client AeroUserProfile is a cache. The Server is the authority on Wallet Balance.
  • Offline: If the player is offline, the "Shop" tab is disabled/hidden. Only "Owned" items can be equipped.

docs.title: 5. Technical Integration
docs.text:
• Data Source: AeroCosmeticResource (Price, Slot, Model).
  • State: AeroUserProfile (Inventory, Wallet).
  • Transaction: aerobeat-tool-api (Handles purchase requests).
  • Scene: LockerRoom.tscn (3D Environment).

docs.title: Athlete Preferences & Overrides
docs.text: This document describes athlete-controlled visual and gameplay comfort settings.

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text: Athletes should be able to preserve comfort and readability without rewriting authored workout content.

docs.title: Visual overrides
docs.text:
• Preferred Environment
  • Avatar Visibility
  • Coach Visibility

docs.title: Menu customization
docs.text: • Main Menu Background

docs.title: Gameplay defaults
docs.text:
• Preferred Mode: Boxing or Flow
  • Preferred View Type: Portal or Track
  • Preferred Difficulty
  • Accessibility Modifiers

docs.title: Scope note
docs.text: Older docs described persistent skin overrides for workout-package gameplay assets. The current product direction is moving away from package-local gameplay asset swaps and toward controlled avatar/cosmetics customization.

docs.title: Athlete Profile Hub
docs.text:
The Athlete Profile is the v1 home for identity, continuity, and simple progress visibility.

It should feel useful the first week without pretending AeroBeat already ships a full social platform or premium analytics suite.

See also:

  • V1 Account and Retention Behavior
  • Gamification & Habit Building
  • Athlete Preferences & Overrides

docs.title: V1 role
docs.text:
The profile hub exists to answer three practical questions:

  1. Who am I in AeroBeat?
  2. What have I done recently?
  3. Am I on track this week?

docs.title: Identity card
docs.text:
V1 should show:

  • display name
  • join date
  • current avatar / equipped official cosmetics
  • current WP balance
  • current streak

V1 does not need public bio fields, social follow counts, or searchable profile pages.

docs.title: Weekly progress card
docs.text:
V1 should show:

  • current weekly goal target
  • progress toward that goal
  • whether this week is already complete
  • current weekly-consistency streak

The progress state should be legible in plain language, such as:

  • 2 / 3 workout days completed
  • Weekly goal complete
  • 4-week streak

docs.title: Lifetime stats card
docs.text:
V1 should keep the stat set narrow and trustworthy:

  • lifetime completed workouts
  • lifetime active minutes
  • lifetime WP earned
  • recent Boxing vs Flow mix if cheap to compute

Avoid trying to impress with a giant dashboard.

docs.title: Recent history
docs.text:
V1 should show a recent activity list with entries like:

  • date/time
  • workout name
  • mode
  • duration
  • WP earned

A short recent list is enough for launch. The point is continuity, not forensic analytics.

docs.title: Preferences entry points
docs.text: The profile hub can link to preferences and the locker room, but it should not become a dumping ground for every settings category.

docs.title: Explicit v1 exclusions
docs.text:
Do not treat these as launch requirements:

  • supporter-only analytics panes
  • health-platform sync
  • CSV export
  • public profile sharing
  • coach heatmaps
  • year/month trend graphs
  • body metrics tracking

Those may become useful later, but they are not part of the retained v1 contract.

docs.title: UX notes
docs.text:
• The profile should be reachable from the main menu and other obvious non-gameplay surfaces.
  • It should load quickly and degrade gracefully if cloud sync is delayed.
  • If the athlete is still a guest, the profile should make the benefits of account conversion obvious without blocking basic free use.

docs.title: ♿ Accessibility Modifiers
docs.text:
Before starting a workout, athletes can enable accessibility-focused modifiers to change gameplay.

  • No Squats: Disables squat prompts.
  • No Weaves: Disables Boxing weave_left and weave_right prompts.
  • No Arrows: Disables Flow arrow-direction validation while keeping timed cell-entry requirements.
  • No Obstacles: Disables Boxing movement prompts such as squat / weave_* and disables Flow obstacle windows.
  • No Bombs: Disables Flow bomb hazards.
  • No Guidance Objects: Hides Flow guidance-only arcs/sliders and similar visual helpers.
  • Zen Mode: Disables scoring pressure so the athlete can just enjoy the sounds and sights.
  • Hit Streak: Enables the hit streak counter to be visible during gameplay.
  • Practice Mode: Enables you to scrub a timeline while paused to jump to sections of the current song during a workout. workout points are not saved while enabled.

docs.title: Difficulty Modifiers
docs.text:
These modifiers tune the challenge of the active gameplay features in this docs slice: Boxing and Flow.

  • Wide Timing Window: widens the hit window beyond the base difficulty timing.
  • Tight Timing Window: narrows the hit window below the base difficulty timing.
  • Ghost Targets: targets disappear as they approach the athlete.
  • Speed Up: targets and obstacles travel faster.
  • Slow Down: targets and obstacles travel slower.
  • Arcade Mode: adds a fail-state health system.
  • Hardcore Mode: mistakes can restart the song or run.

Future gameplay features may eventually define additional modifier behavior, but Step and Dance are not active gameplay modes in this docs slice.

docs.title: Community Edition
docs.text: The Community Edition is the first public-facing AeroBeat product path.

docs.title: Release priority
docs.text:
1. PC community first
  2. mobile second
  3. VR third

docs.title: Community Edition scope for this slice
docs.text:
• free-to-play app distribution
  • official gameplay features: Boxing and Flow
  • official gameplay input: camera only
  • a workout catalog containing both free workouts and premium workouts
  • offline-capable installed workouts once trust/access rules are satisfied
  • workout browsing, favorites, recommendations, and progression surfaces
  • AeroBeat-owned account architecture for profile/progression/library systems, even if some end-user account UX is phased

docs.title: Platform wording
docs.text:
The docs should stop implying present-tense parity across PC, mobile, and VR.

  • PC is the main release target for the first community slice
  • mobile remains an important follow-on target
  • VR remains a later platform return, not the lead product shape

docs.title: Free workouts
docs.text: Free workouts are the on-ramp for the community edition. They should be discoverable without requiring the premium purchase path, while still respecting AeroBeat trust, compatibility, and install policy.

docs.title: Premium workouts
docs.text:
Premium workouts are paid content within the community ecosystem.

For the locked v1 strategy:

  • purchases must follow official platform/store paths
  • mod.io remains the current community/distribution layer
  • athlete-facing UX should still speak in AeroBeat ownership/access terms rather than raw provider wallet language
  • every premium package is one difficulty only
  • premium pricing follows $1 per 10 minutes, rounded up to the nearest whole dollar
  • counted runtime includes coaching media when coaching is enabled

docs.title: Public release gate
docs.text:
For launch, all public UGC — free and premium — goes through mod.io Full Curation before release.

That means:

  • creators do not publish directly to the public catalog without review
  • review is expected for first publication
  • review is also expected again for material updates
  • the approved live version stays up while a replacement version is under review unless the live version itself is under investigation

docs.title: Lane conversion rules
docs.text:
Free/premium lane conversion is not allowed in place.

  • free → premium must be submitted as a new premium item
  • premium → free must be submitted as a new free item

If the new submission is effectively the same workout as the prior opposite-lane listing, reviewers may require the old version to be removed.

See also: Premium Workout Governance and V1 UGC Submission and Review Policy.

docs.title: Funding and progression
docs.text:
AeroBeat may still use supporter/community programs where helpful, but the main product framing should distinguish between:

  • supporter/community perks
  • premium workout access
  • long-term account-driven progression such as workout points, profile state, and cosmetics unlocks

Future customization should point toward controlled avatar/cosmetics progression via workout points rather than old package-asset mod examples.

docs.title: Digital Stores & Arcade Editions
docs.text: These release paths remain valid planning spaces, but they are not the first shipping priority.

docs.title: Current sequencing
docs.text:
• PC community edition comes first
  • mobile store work comes after the PC community release
  • VR and deeper arcade variants come later

docs.title: Commercial release direction
docs.text:
If AeroBeat later targets digital storefronts or arcades, those editions may diverge from the community build in licensing, content curation, and distribution requirements.

Important wording note: app-store and mobile UGC constraints are real considerations, but the docs should keep them soft for now. They are planning pressures, not yet absolute blockers.

docs.title: Likely differences from the community edition
docs.text:
• more curated content pipelines
  • tighter commercial licensing for music and brand collaborations
  • platform-specific UX and packaging decisions
  • more controlled moderation/distribution surfaces

docs.title: Product framing
docs.text: This page should be read as future release planning, not a promise of simultaneous storefront parity.

docs.title: 🔮 Future Roadmap
docs.text: These will likely change as production continues, but they reflect the current product priorities more accurately than the older broad roadmap.

docs.title: Launch path
docs.text: • Phase 1 (Community Launch): PC-first free-to-play release with camera-based Boxing + Flow, free workouts, premium workouts, AeroBeat-owned account identity, entitlement/library sync, Workout Points, and simple weekly goals/streaks.

docs.title: Recommended post-launch order
docs.text:
• Phase 2 (Async Retention): scoped leaderboards, personal-best comparison, and other low-risk asynchronous competition layers.
  • Phase 3 (Light Social): friends/following, challenge sharing, and lightweight accountability features.
  • Phase 4 (Progression Expansion): deeper official cosmetic unlocks and stronger Workout Points sinks once earn/spend balance is validated.
  • Phase 5 (Group Features): crews or similar group-accountability systems.
  • Phase 6 (Multiplayer): real-time multiplayer or ghost competition after the solo and async loops are proven.
  • Phase 7 (Live Ops Layers): premium currency, seasonal progression, battle passes, and rotating stores only if the product has the audience and cadence to justify them.

docs.title: Still future-facing
docs.text:
• mobile and VR platform expansion
  • additional gameplay cores beyond Boxing and Flow
  • stricter platform-specific premium UGC variants for console, arcade, or commercial deployments

docs.title: Social Crews (Guilds)
docs.text: Crews are the social backbone of AeroBeat. They allow athletes to form communities, compete on private leaderboards, and find workout partners.

docs.title: 1. Philosophy
docs.text:
• Community First: Crews are about belonging, not just competition.
  • Accountability: Crews provide a structure for athletes to motivate each other, share workouts, and celebrate milestones together.
  • Open Access: While creating a Crew is a Supporter perk, joining one is free for everyone. This prevents the community from fracturing into "Paid vs Free" groups.

docs.title: A. Creation (The Founder)
docs.text:
• Requirement: Must be an active Supporter.
  • Cost: None (included in Supporter perks).
  • Limits: One Crew ownership per account.

docs.title: B. Identity
docs.text:
• Name: Unique string (e.g., "Neon Runners"). Max 24 chars.
  • Tag: Unique 3-4 character ticker (e.g., [NEON]). Displayed next to member names in lobbies.
  • Banner: Customizable background color/pattern for the Crew page.

docs.title: C. Roles
docs.text:
1. Captain (Owner): Can manage settings, kick members, promote officers.
  2. Officer: Can invite/kick members, update the "Message of the Day".
  3. Member: Can view private leaderboards and chat.

docs.title: A. Private Leaderboards
docs.text: Every song in the game gets a "Crew Filter" on the leaderboard. This allows members to compete specifically against their friends rather than the global top 10.

docs.title: B. The Gym (Hub)
docs.text:
A dedicated UI page for the Crew.

  • Message of the Day (MOTD): Pinned announcement from the Captain. This is the primary way to specify workouts for the group to focus on for the week.
  • Roster: List of members and their online status.
  • Activity Feed: "UserX just set a new PB on SongY!" Members can click a "Kudos" button to congratulate each other, fostering accountability.

docs.title: C. Connectivity
docs.text:
Finding friends in a sea of players can be hard.

  • Lobby Highlighting: Crew members appear with a distinct visual flair in the Lobby Browser, making it easy to find and join their sessions.

docs.title: D. Capacity
docs.text:
• Level 1: 50 Members.
  • Expansion: Crews can level up by earning collective WP (Workout Points), unlocking higher caps (up to 500).

docs.title: 4. Moderation & Safety
docs.text:
To prevent toxicity, strict rules apply to Crew metadata.

  • Profanity Filter: Names and Tags pass through a strict server-side filter.
  • Reporting: Players can report a Crew for offensive content.
  • Sanctions:
    • Strike 1: Name reset + Warning.
    • Strike 2: Disbandment + Captain Ban.

docs.title: Database Schema (Concept)
docs.text:
TABLE Crews (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(24) UNIQUE,
    tag VARCHAR(4) UNIQUE,
    owner_id UUID REFERENCES Users(id),
    level INT DEFAULT 1,
    xp BIGINT DEFAULT 0
);

TABLE CrewMembers (
    crew_id UUID REFERENCES Crews(id),
    user_id UUID REFERENCES Users(id),
    role ENUM('member', 'officer', 'captain'),
    joined_at TIMESTAMP
);

docs.title: Supporter Logic
docs.text:
• Creation: API checks user.is_supporter before INSERT INTO Crews.
  • Lapse: If a Captain's Supporter status expires, the Crew remains active. They just cannot edit settings (Name/Tag) until they renew. They do not lose ownership.

docs.title: Community Creations
docs.text: AeroBeat still aims to support community-facing content, but the current docs slice is explicit about the default imported-player publishing model.

docs.title: Durable content hierarchy
docs.text:
• Song Package → one imported source song/root that may contain multiple playable slices
  • Song → reusable audio and timing source inside that package
  • Chart → one concrete playable feature+difficulty slice
  • Set → exact playable record linking one Song and one Chart
  • Playlist → future multi-song grouping above song packages

docs.title: Community content types kept in this slice
docs.text:
• Song packages
  • Songs
  • Charts / Sets for Boxing and Flow
  • Playlists as the future multi-song grouping concept
  • Environments as a sibling system outside default song packages
  • Customization as profile/account-level avatar + cosmetics work rather than package-local swaps

docs.title: Default imported-player rules
docs.text:
The following rules are the current default direction:

  • one song package may contain multiple charts/difficulties for the same imported song root
  • each chart is still one exact playable slice
  • multi-song grouping should use playlists, not workout-package bundles
  • coaching is not part of the default imported-player package contract
  • environment choice is outside the default imported-player package contract
  • all public-facing product truth should stay aligned to the BeatSaver conversion direction

docs.title: Building a playable song package
docs.text:
Creators/tools/importers assemble playable content by choosing:

  • gameplay feature: Boxing or Flow
  • exact chart difficulty
  • exact song + chart pairing through a set
  • optional future playlist sequencing above many song packages

docs.title: Athlete overrides
docs.text:
Athletes may still want profile-level preferences such as:

  • avatar identity
  • preferred environment style
  • accessibility and comfort settings

Those account-level choices are different from the old package-local gameplay asset swap model.

docs.title: What changed
docs.text:
Older docs taught workout packages, package-local coaching, and package-local environments much more aggressively as current truth. That is no longer the default imported-player story.

Future customization direction should instead point toward:

  • controlled avatar customization
  • cosmetics unlocks
  • profile-driven progression using workout points

docs.title: User Content Overview
docs.text: AeroBeat separates gameplay logic from authored/imported content so community contributions and imported catalog material can stay inspectable and tool-friendly.

docs.title: What the default imported path currently owns
docs.text:
• song packages
  • songs
  • charts
  • sets
  • future playlists above song packages

docs.title: What the default imported path does not assume
docs.text:
This docs pass removes the older workout-package-heavy model from default current truth.

That means imported content should no longer be described as normally carrying:

  • one-difficulty workout-package boundaries
  • package-local coaching by default
  • package-local environment ownership by default
  • package-local gameplay asset swap bundles

docs.title: Environment direction
docs.text: Environment choice still matters, but for the default imported-player path it is treated as player/system state outside the song package.

docs.title: Customization direction going forward
docs.text:
Customization still matters, but the more likely product path is:

  • account-level avatar identity
  • cosmetics
  • progression and unlocks via workout points

That direction is more controlled and store-friendly than the older freeform package-local asset model.

docs.title: Policing Content
docs.text: AeroBeat is an open platform, but it is not a lawless one. The locked v1 moderation posture is mod.io curation plus human review, with automation used as advisory support rather than as an AI-first public gate.

docs.title: V1 moderation posture
docs.text:
For launch:

  • all public UGC must pass review before release
  • the operational gate is mod.io Full Curation
  • human reviewers remain responsible for approval decisions
  • automation may help surface risks, but it should not be treated as the only authority

This means v1 moderation is intentionally lower-scope than a custom AeroBeat-owned review platform.

docs.title: What review is trying to catch
docs.text:
Review is meant to catch:

  • malformed or broken packages
  • missing required assets
  • misleading metadata, thumbnails, or storefront claims
  • free/premium lane rule violations
  • clone or near-duplicate abuse
  • rights / licensing self-attestation problems
  • illegal, abusive, unsafe, deceptive, or otherwise disallowed content

Review should not be framed as a vague aesthetic-quality gate.

docs.title: Rights, copyright, and takedowns
docs.text:
Creators must self-attest that they hold the necessary rights for all included content and assets.

The v1 posture is:

  • no proof-of-rights documentation required in the normal submission path
  • the current operational route for copyright / DMCA / takedown handling is mod.io's reporting and moderation system
  • repeat or severe abuse may still lead to creator sanctions or removal from trusted lanes

This describes the current review/takedown workflow, not a final legal conclusion that the full DMCA / safe-harbor posture is settled. Depending on final provider/legal guidance, AeroBeat may still need additional escalation or response duties when likely infringement is reported or independently discovered.

docs.title: Suspensions and post-publication action
docs.text:
The v1 policy vocabulary still needs a conceptual suspended state for high-risk cases after release.

Examples include:

  • DMCA / rights complaints
  • fraud or bait-and-switch behavior
  • serious safety or trust concerns
  • severe clone / abuse concerns

In v1, that concept maps to mod.io deactivated rather than a separate AeroBeat-owned moderation state machine.

docs.title: Reporting content
docs.text:
Athletes should be able to report content from the in-game browser or from the mod.io-facing listing/reporting flows.

Human review remains the durable decision layer for escalations, ambiguous policy calls, and repeat-offender handling.

docs.title: Streamer mode note
docs.text: Streamer-mode and copyright-safe playback ideas may still be useful product features, but they are separate from the locked v1 moderation gate. They should not be confused with the public submission/review policy.

docs.title: Accessibility Guide
docs.text: AeroBeat is intended to be approachable for a wide range of athletes, and the current v1 slice should keep that goal grounded in the camera-first product reality.

docs.title: Core accessibility principles
docs.text:
• athletes should be able to scale intensity safely
  • charts should leave room for substitution and reduced range of motion
  • calibration should adapt to the athlete, not the other way around

docs.title: Current focus
docs.text:
The official gameplay-accessibility story should be written first around camera-driven Boxing and Flow.

UI navigation may still use mouse on PC and touch on mobile, but those should not be confused with official non-camera gameplay support.

docs.title: Useful modifier examples
docs.text:
• no squats
  • no knee strikes
  • reduced leg-lift demand
  • no obstacles/body-movement prompts
  • simplified Flow guidance
  • zen or low-pressure play modes

docs.title: Future-support note
docs.text: Adaptive controllers, gamepad variants, keyboard workflows, and XR comfort paths may still matter later, but they should be presented as future support or accessibility exploration rather than current official gameplay parity.

docs.title: Creating Custom Avatars
docs.text:
Avatars in AeroBeat primarily support:

  1. Coach presentation
  2. Athlete identity in social or multiplayer contexts

This guide covers how to rig, import, and configure custom 3D characters.

docs.title: Scope note
docs.text: Avatar customization remains a valid future-facing product area and fits the newer direction of controlled profile/cosmetics progression better than the older package-local gameplay asset model.

docs.title: Calibration Wizard
docs.text: AeroBeat calibration should now be read through a camera-first lens.

docs.title: Official gameplay calibration in this slice
docs.text: The primary calibration flow is for camera gameplay plus normal audio/video sync.

docs.title: Global latency
docs.text:
• audio offset calibration
  • video offset calibration

docs.title: Camera calibration
docs.text:
• ensure face and hands are well lit
  • keep enough distance for upper-body framing
  • use a stable background when possible
  • calibrate reach/pose from the athlete's actual play position

docs.title: Navigation vs gameplay
docs.text: UI navigation may still use mouse on PC and touch on mobile, but that is separate from the official v1 gameplay-input story.

docs.title: Future-input note
docs.text: JoyCon, gamepad, keyboard, and XR-specific calibration flows may still be worth documenting later, but they should be treated as future support rather than official v1 gameplay requirements.

docs.title: Seated play
docs.text: Seated camera calibration remains important. Athletes should calibrate from the posture they will actually use during play so reach and head-position expectations stay comfortable.

docs.title: Contributing Workflow
docs.text:
Not a Programmer?

If you are an Artist, Musician, or Choreographer, this workflow might be too technical. Check out the Contributing Section on the homepage to find the right guide for you.

This guide outlines the lifecycle of a code contribution to the AeroBeat platform. Follow these steps to ensure your Pull Request (PR) is accepted quickly.

docs.title: 1. Fork & Clone
docs.text:
1. Fork the target repository (e.g., aerobeat-feature-boxing) to your GitHub account.
  2. Clone your fork locally. 
    git clone https://github.com/YourUsername/aerobeat-feature-boxing.git

docs.title: 2. Setup Environment
docs.text:
Before writing code, you must initialize the dependencies.

  1. Run Setup: 

    python setup_dev.py
    This downloads the lane-appropriate core repos (for example aerobeat-input-core, aerobeat-feature-core, aerobeat-content-core, aerobeat-ui-core, aerobeat-tool-core, or aerobeat-asset-core) and other dev dependencies into the .testbed/addons/ folder (or addons/ for Assembly).

  2. Open in Godot:

    • Features/UI/Input: Open the project inside .testbed/.
    • Assembly: Open the project at the root.

docs.title: 3. Create a Branch
docs.text:
Create a descriptive branch for your changes.

git checkout -b feature/new-scoring-algo
# or
git checkout -b fix/input-latency

docs.title: 4. Development & Testing
docs.text:
1. Write Code: Follow the Style Guide.
  2. Write Tests: Create unit tests in test/unit/.
  3. Run Tests: Use the GUT panel in Godot to ensure all tests pass.
    • Requirement: We enforce 100% Code Coverage for logic repositories.

docs.title: 5. Submit Pull Request (PR)
docs.text:
1. Push your branch to your fork.
  2. Open a Pull Request against the main branch of the upstream repository.
  3. Description: Clearly explain what you changed and why. Link to any relevant Issues.

docs.title: 6. The Quality Gates
docs.text: Once your PR is open, our automated bots will check your code.

docs.title: 🤖 CLA Assistant (Legal)
docs.text:
• Check: Verifies you have signed the Contributor License Agreement.
  • Action: If this is your first time, a bot will comment on your PR. You must reply with: > "I have read and agree to the terms of the AeroBeat-Workouts CLA."

docs.title: 🧪 CI/CD (Technical)
docs.text:
• Check: Runs the GUT test suite in a headless Godot runner.
  • Action: If any test fails or coverage drops below 100%, the check will fail. You must fix the errors and push again.

docs.title: 7. Review & Merge
docs.text:
1. Code Review: A maintainer will review your code for architecture and style compliance.
  2. Merge: Once approved and all checks pass, your code will be merged into main.

docs.title: Cosmetics
docs.text:
This page is intentionally narrow.

AeroBeat cosmetics are currently documented as a controlled customization lane, not as a public mod-pack system.

docs.title: Current truth
docs.text:
Use cosmetics wording that stays inside these boundaries:

  • cosmetics are presentation-only
  • cosmetics do not alter gameplay rules or judged motion
  • cosmetics are separate from workout-package authoring
  • cosmetics are separate from any old manifest-era mod-pack story

For adjacent current-truth context, see:

  • Account, Identity, and Entitlements
  • UGC & Modding Architecture
  • Content Model
  • Avatar Creation

docs.title: What counts as a cosmetic
docs.text:
Typical examples:

  • hats
  • glasses
  • masks
  • backpacks
  • small wearable accessories
  • other non-gameplay avatar presentation items

docs.title: Non-negotiable rule
docs.text:
Cosmetics stay cosmetic.

A cosmetic should never:

  • change hit logic
  • change chart semantics
  • change input validation
  • change authored workout compatibility
  • require arbitrary runtime scripting or code injection
  • hide or block critical workout readability

docs.title: Product-direction wording
docs.text:
If another page mentions cosmetics, describe them as one or more of:

  • profile-driven customization
  • curated or official unlocks
  • progression or entitlement-linked presentation items
  • controlled avatar-expression features

Do not describe them as:

  • freeform public manifest-based cosmetic packs
  • equal-status public mod SDK surfaces
  • package-local gameplay asset swaps as the main creator workflow

docs.title: Prototype-safe authoring guidance
docs.text: Even before the final public contract exists, these constraints are still sensible:

docs.title: Geometry
docs.text:
• keep meshes lightweight
  • keep silhouettes readable
  • use stable pivots/origins for attachment
  • author at sane real-world scale

docs.title: Materials
docs.text:
• prefer simple performant materials
  • keep texture sizes modest
  • minimize material count and draw-call overhead

docs.title: Attachment behavior
docs.text:
• target stable avatar attachment points
  • expect per-avatar fit adjustments
  • avoid designs that depend on custom gameplay logic

docs.title: What this page intentionally does not define
docs.text:
This page is not the place for final public-contract details such as:

  • exact package shape
  • exact resource class names
  • exact socket or bone naming
  • uploader/validator UI requirements
  • store/economy metadata schema

Those should only be documented when the owning runtime and package contracts are current enough to be trustworthy.

docs.title: Bottom line
docs.text:
Cosmetics are a controlled presentation system.

They are not the public workout-package contract, and they are not evidence that AeroBeat currently ships a broad public mod-loader.

docs.title: Feature Development Guide
docs.text: This guide is for gameplay engineers building AeroBeat features.

docs.title: Current product focus
docs.text:
The active product slice is centered on:

  • Boxing
  • Flow
  • camera-first gameplay

The architecture may still support future features later, but feature-development docs should not imply Dance or Step are active product modes today.

docs.title: What a feature owns
docs.text:
A feature is a self-contained library that provides:

  1. gameplay logic
  2. stable runtime/view-facing scenes or resources
  3. choreography parsing for that feature's authored charts

It does not provide main-menu UI, hardware input providers, or player-profile storage.

docs.title: Stability rule
docs.text: Feature-facing contracts should stay stable enough that downstream visuals and tooling do not break when implementation details change.

docs.title: Creator Quickstart Guide
docs.text: Welcome to the AeroBeat creator/importer community.

docs.title: Before you start
docs.text:
Read the current canonical conversion and content-contract docs first:

  • BeatSaver to AeroBeat Flow v1 Conversion
  • BeatSaver to AeroBeat Boxing v1 Conversion
  • Content Model
  • User Content Overview

Those pages describe the active direction:

  • imported song packages
  • multiple charts/difficulties under one song root
  • playlists for future multi-song grouping
  • environments outside song packages by default
  • no default coaching/portal baggage in the imported-player contract

docs.title: Creator lanes in this slice
docs.text:
• importers/converters turn source maps into AeroBeat song packages
  • choreographers refine Boxing and Flow chart behavior where needed
  • tool authors improve conversion, validation, inspection, and packaging workflows
  • environment authors work in sibling environment systems rather than inside every imported song package by default

docs.title: Current sanity checklist
docs.text:
Before presenting a docs change or implementation as current truth, make sure that:

  • the package is described as a song package, not a one-difficulty workout package
  • multiple charts/difficulties are allowed under one imported song root
  • multi-song grouping uses playlist language
  • environment choice is not taught as package-owned default truth
  • coaching is not taught as required default imported-package truth
  • the docs stay aligned with the canonical BeatSaver conversion pages

docs.title: Customization note
docs.text: Older docs talked much more heavily about package-local gameplay skins/assets. That is no longer the main content story in this slice. Future customization is more likely to live in controlled avatar/cosmetics systems tied to progression.

docs.title: Skins in the Current Docs Slice
docs.text: AeroBeat still has a skin lane, but it should no longer be read as a public v1 creator pillar or as the old package-local gameplay-swap story.

docs.title: Current truth
docs.text:
• Official v1 gameplay: camera-driven Boxing and Flow on PC first
  • Current customization emphasis: controlled avatar / cosmetics / profile progression, not community gameplay-skin swapping
  • Skin repo role: internal or controlled product presentation work in the asset lane
  • Canonical template: aerobeat-template-skin

If you are looking for the main community creation path today, start with workout packages, choreography, coaching, and environments instead.

docs.title: What this page means now
docs.text:
Use the skin lane when AeroBeat itself needs to author or validate:

  • internal glove/hand presentation variants for Boxing
  • internal target/obstacle presentation variants for Boxing or Flow
  • trail/accent presentation for retained gameplay features
  • controlled product-facing visual variants that belong to the asset lane

Do not use this page to imply that AeroBeat v1 ships a broad public marketplace for swapping gameplay equipment skins.

docs.title: Canonical starting point
docs.text:
The runnable template story now lives in:

  • aerobeat-template-skin
  • aerobeat-docs/templates/skins/README.md for the docs-side handoff note

That template already captures the current repo truth:

  • skins are internal/system-facing packages by default
  • shared contracts belong in aerobeat-asset-core
  • concrete feature dependencies should stay explicit and selective
  • the old universal mod/SDK story should not be revived accidentally

docs.title: What belongs in skin work
docs.text:
• visual meshes/materials for retained feature presentation
  • asset-lane resources consumed by a concrete feature or assembly
  • validation against the current Boxing/Flow runtime surfaces when needed

docs.title: What does not belong here
docs.text:
• community-authored workout package content
  • coach content
  • avatar/cosmetic progression design as a substitute for gameplay skins
  • claims that skins are an equal-status public modding lane in the current v1 product slice
  • old AeroModManifest / public uploader instructions from the superseded UGC-first docs story

docs.title: Practical workflow
docs.text:
1. Open aerobeat-template-skin.
  2. Restore the .testbed/ GodotEnv dependencies documented there.
  3. Validate the presentation assets against the concrete feature surface that actually consumes them.
  4. Keep the package boundary and dependency list narrow.
  5. If the work is really avatar/cosmetic/environment content instead, route it to the correct lane instead of forcing it into skins.

docs.title: Creator routing note
docs.text:
If you are a community creator trying to decide where to contribute today:

  • build workouts/charts/sets/coaching/environments for the active content lane
  • treat skins as a controlled/internal asset path unless a future docs update explicitly broadens that scope

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Skins are still real, but in the current docs slice they are not the headline community customization system. Read them as a narrow asset-lane package for controlled product presentation, aligned to the retained Boxing + Flow v1 scope.

docs.title: Creating Boxing Choreography
docs.text: Boxing is a core retained AeroBeat gameplay feature and should be authored for the current camera-first product slice.

docs.title: Authoring priorities
docs.text:
• keep movement readable on camera
  • favor safe, repeatable workout motion
  • optimize first for the PC community release path
  • align example/chart vocabulary with the locked BeatSaver Boxing v1 conversion contract

docs.title: Chart shape
docs.text:
A Boxing chart is a reusable Chart record. The song owns audio/timing, the chart owns one concrete playable difficulty, and a package-local Set links the chart to the selected song, environment, and optional coaching overlay.

For the current first-pass canonical Boxing vocabulary, use flat beats: entries built from:

  • required start
  • optional inclusive end
  • required concrete type

Current contract-aligned Boxing beat families are:

  • straight_left / straight_right
  • hook_left / hook_right
  • uppercut_left / uppercut_right
  • guard
  • squat
  • weave_left / weave_right

For this docs pass:

  • do not treat portal as part of the current Boxing chart contract
  • do not teach punch_left / punch_right, knee_*, leg_lift_*, sidestep_*, or run_in_place as current canonical Boxing chart vocabulary
  • keep the Boxing chart semantic and runtime-focused rather than source-shaped

docs.title: BeatSaver conversion reference
docs.text:
If you need the canonical import/conversion rules for deriving Boxing charts from BeatSaver source data, use:

  • BeatSaver to AeroBeat Boxing v1 Conversion

This choreography page stays focused on authoring guidance rather than source-conversion policy.

docs.title: Scope note
docs.text: Portal-aware or VR-oriented presentation ideas can still exist as future runtime concerns, but they should not be taught as part of the current Boxing chart contract.

docs.title: Creating Flow Choreography
docs.text: Flow is a retained AeroBeat gameplay feature, but the old portal-driven authored chart model is no longer the current truth for the camera-first product slice.

docs.title: Authoring priorities
docs.text:
• preserve readable continuous motion on camera
  • keep movement safe and well telegraphed
  • optimize for the PC-first community release path
  • stay honest about the direct calibrated 4x3 gameplay direction instead of re-teaching the older normalized Flow vocabulary

docs.title: Current direction
docs.text:
Flow is now aimed at a direct calibrated 4x3 camera-space model.

That means current docs should think in terms of:

  • a fixed athlete-relative 4x3 grid locked from calibration
  • left/right wrist entry into exact target cells
  • arrow-note direction judged from recent wrist motion, not from old authored portal / placement semantics
  • bombs as wrist-space avoid objects
  • obstacles as nose-space occupancy bans
  • arcs/sliders as guidance-only visuals in v1

docs.title: Chart-shape note for this pass
docs.text:
The old docs taught Flow as a flat beats: list with portal, placement, and follow-through direction as the canonical authored model. That is now stale and should not be treated as the current contract.

The only Flow object shape already frozen tightly enough to teach here is the first-pass burst beat object:

  • required start
  • required end
  • required type: burst
  • required hand (left / right)
  • required head placement
  • required head direction
  • required tailPlacement
  • required checkpointCount
  • optional spacingBias

For ordinary Flow notes/objects, the durable YAML encoding is still being rewritten around the direct 4x3 model. Until that lands, do not author new canonical examples around old portal, swing_*, trail_*, warn_*, or reward_* chart vocabulary.

docs.title: BeatSaver conversion reference
docs.text:
If you need the canonical import/conversion rules for deriving Flow charts from BeatSaver source data, use:

  • BeatSaver to AeroBeat Flow v1 Conversion

This choreography page stays focused on authoring guidance rather than source-conversion policy.

docs.title: Scope note
docs.text: Flow still needs future-friendly rendering flexibility, but the official docs should stay grounded in camera gameplay first and should not preserve superseded portal-era contract language as fallback guidance.

docs.title: Choreography Overview
docs.text: In AeroBeat, charting is the art of translating music into movement.

docs.title: Active choreography scope
docs.text:
The current docs set teaches choreography for the two retained gameplay features:

  • Boxing
  • Flow

Dance and Step are no longer active gameplay features in this documentation slice.

docs.title: Content model
docs.text:
AeroBeat choreography still fits the same durable chain:

  • Song → Chart → Set → Workout

docs.title: View and platform guidance
docs.text:
Creators should optimize first for the current official product slice:

  • camera-driven gameplay
  • PC-first release

Portal-aware presentation and broader platform rendering can stay in mind as future-friendly design constraints, but they should not be documented as equal-status v1 product targets.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. choose a song
  2. author or edit one Boxing or Flow chart
  3. wire that chart into a set
  4. validate readability, movement safety, and timing
  5. publish the package content through the appropriate tooling

docs.title: Refund & Cancellation Policy
docs.text:
Last Updated: [Date]

AeroBeat operates on a "Support, Don't Buy" model. Our "Supporter" tags are voluntary contributions to help fund server costs and open-source development. Because of this, our refund policy differs from traditional subscription services.

docs.title: 1. Nature of Purchase
docs.text:
• Donation-Based: Payments made for Supporter status are classified as voluntary donations with incidental benefits.
  • Non-Recurring: All purchases are for a fixed period (e.g., 1 Month, 1 Year). We do not store your card for auto-renewal. You will never be charged automatically.

docs.title: 2. General Refund Policy
docs.text:
All sales are final.

Because Supporter status provides immediate access to server-side benefits (Cloud Storage, API usage) and is treated as a donation to the project, we generally do not offer refunds for "change of mind" or "unused time".

docs.title: 3. Exceptional Circumstances
docs.text: We understand that errors happen. We will consider refund requests under the following specific circumstances:

docs.title: A. Technical Errors
docs.text: If a technical glitch caused a duplicate charge or if you were charged for a tier you did not select, we will issue a full refund for the erroneous transaction immediately.

docs.title: B. Fraudulent Activity
docs.text: If your card was used without your permission, please contact your bank to initiate a dispute, or contact us immediately to reverse the charge before it settles.

docs.title: C. Long-Term Pro-Ration (Case-by-Case)
docs.text:
For purchases of 1 Year or longer, if you are unable to use the service due to permanent hardware failure or medical reasons, you may request a pro-rated refund for the unused months.

  • Calculation: Refund = (Total Paid) - (Months Used * Monthly Rate).
  • Note: The "Months Used" are calculated at the standard $4.00/mo rate, not the discounted yearly rate.

docs.title: 4. Account Terminations
docs.text: If your account is suspended or banned due to a violation of our Code of Conduct or Terms of Service (e.g., cheating, hate speech, uploading malicious content), you forfeit all remaining Supporter time. No refunds will be issued for banned accounts.

docs.title: 5. How to Request a Refund
docs.text:
To submit a request, please email aerobeat-workouts@gmail.com with:

  1. Your Username.
  2. Transaction ID (from the receipt).
  3. Reason for the request.

We aim to respond to all billing inquiries within 48 hours.

docs.title: Licensing Overview
docs.text:
At AeroBeat, we believe in Open Source, but we also respect that the reality of Game Development involves proprietary hardware, third-party assets, and commercial partnerships.

We use a Hybrid Licensing Strategy to balance these needs. This section explains exactly what you can do, what you must share, and how to safely contribute.

docs.title: Quick Reference Table
docs.text: Repository Prefix License Can I Close Source? Proprietary Strategy aerobeat-input-core MPL 2.0 Only your new files. Link / Import aerobeat-feature-core MPL 2.0 Only your new files. Link / Import aerobeat-content-core MPL 2.0 Only your new files. Link / Import aerobeat-asset-core MPL 2.0 Only your new files.
