docs.title: AeroBeat-Workouts Individual Contributor License Agreement ("CLA")
docs.text:
Thank you for your interest in contributing to AeroBeat!

To clarify the intellectual property license granted with Contributions from any person or entity, AeroBeat-Workouts ("We" or "Us") must have a Contributor License Agreement ("CLA") on file that has been signed by each Contributor, indicating agreement to the license terms below.

This license is for your protection as a Contributor as well as for the protection of AeroBeat-Workouts and its users; it does not change your rights to use your own Contributions for any other purpose.

docs.title: 1. Definitions
docs.text:
• "You" (or "Your") shall mean the copyright owner or legal entity authorized by the copyright owner that is making this Agreement with AeroBeat-Workouts.
  • "Contribution" shall mean any original work of authorship, including any modifications or additions to an existing work, that is intentionally submitted by You to AeroBeat-Workouts for inclusion in, or documentation of, any of the products owned or managed by AeroBeat-Workouts (the "Work").
  • "Submit" means any form of electronic, verbal, or written communication sent to AeroBeat-Workouts or its representatives, including but not limited to communication on electronic mailing lists, source code control systems (GitHub), and issue tracking systems.

docs.title: 2. Grant of Copyright License
docs.text:
Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, You hereby grant to AeroBeat-Workouts and to recipients of software distributed by AeroBeat-Workouts a perpetual, worldwide, non-exclusive, no-charge, royalty-free, irrevocable copyright license to reproduce, prepare derivative works of, publicly display, publicly perform, sublicense, and distribute Your Contributions and such derivative works.

Why do we need this?

This grant allows us to:

  1. Distribute the Community Edition under GPLv3.
  2. Distribute Commercial/Arcade Editions under Proprietary Licenses (Dual Licensing).
  3. Defend the project against copyright infringement.

docs.title: 3. Grant of Patent License
docs.text: Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, You hereby grant to AeroBeat-Workouts and to recipients of software distributed by AeroBeat-Workouts a perpetual, worldwide, non-exclusive, no-charge, royalty-free, irrevocable (except as stated in this section) patent license to make, have made, use, offer to sell, sell, import, and otherwise transfer the Work.

docs.title: 4. You Represent That:
docs.text:
1. You are legally entitled to grant the above license.
  2. Your Contribution is your original creation.
  3. Your Contribution includes complete details of any third-party license or other restriction (including, but not limited to, related patents and trademarks) of which you are personally aware and which are associated with any part of Your Contribution.

docs.title: 5. No Other Rights
docs.text: You reserve all right, title, and interest in and to Your Contributions. This CLA does not transfer ownership of your code to AeroBeat-Workouts; it only grants us a license to use it.

docs.title: 6. How to Sign
docs.text:
To sign this CLA, please add the following line to your Pull Request description or post it as a comment in the PR:

"I have read and agree to the terms of the AeroBeat-Workouts CLA."

docs.title: Contributing to AeroBeat
docs.text:
We welcome contributions of all kinds! Whether you are fixing a bug in the Core, designing a new environment, or writing an input driver for a new hardware device.

  1. Check the License: Please review our Licensing Overview to understand how your contributions will be protected.
  2. Pick a Repo: Navigate to the specific repository relevant to your skill set.
  3. Sign the CLA: To ensure we can protect the project and support commercial ports (which fund the project), we require a Contributor License Agreement for code contributions.
  4. Run Setup: Use the included ./setup_dev script to initialize your local environment.

Current Status: AeroBeat is currently in Prototype (v0.0.1). We are actively building the Core Contracts and the initial Boxing Feature.

docs.title: Attribution Guide
docs.text: If you fork AeroBeat or use our assets, you must provide proper credit. Here is how to do it correctly.

docs.title: 1. In Your Source Code
docs.text:
• Do Not Remove Headers: You must keep the existing copyright headers in all source files (GPLv3/MPL 2.0 requirement).
  • Add Your Own: If you modify a file, add your own copyright line below ours. 
    # Copyright (c) 2026 AeroBeat-Workouts
# Copyright (c) 2027 Your Name/Company

docs.title: 2. In Your Game Client
docs.text:
If you release a build (even a non-commercial one), you must credit AeroBeat in the user interface.

  • Credits Screen: Include "Powered by AeroBeat" or "Based on AeroBeat Platform".
  • Assets: If you use our CC BY-NC 4.0 assets, list "AeroBeat-Workouts" in your art credits.

docs.title: For Commercial Partners
docs.text: While our community releases are strictly GPLv3, AeroBeat-Workouts retains the copyright to the codebase. This allows us to offer Dual Licensing for commercial partners who need to bypass the restrictions of the GPL.

docs.title: What is Dual Licensing?
docs.text:
Dual Licensing means the same code is available under two different licenses:

  1. Open Source (GPLv3): Free to use, but requires you to share your source code if you distribute it.
  2. Commercial (Proprietary): A paid license that allows you to keep your source code private, modify the engine freely, and deploy to closed platforms (like Consoles or Arcades).

docs.title: Why would I need a Commercial License?
docs.text:
• Closed Source Requirements: You are building a proprietary game (e.g., "Linkin Park Arcade Edition") and cannot share the source code for your specific gameplay logic or UI Shell.
  • Console NDAs: Sony, Nintendo, and Microsoft SDKs are under NDA and cannot be mixed with GPL open-source code repositories.
  • Legal Safety: You want a traditional software license agreement with indemnification, rather than relying on a community license.

docs.title: How it Works
docs.text:
Because all contributors sign a Contributor License Agreement (CLA), AeroBeat-Workouts has the legal right to re-license the codebase.

  1. Contact Us: Reach out to discuss your project scope.
  2. License Grant: We provide a specific license grant for the repositories you need (e.g., aerobeat-assembly-*, aerobeat-ui-shell-*).
  3. Development: You fork the repositories privately. You can pull updates from the Open Source upstream, but you are not required to push your changes back.
  4. Funding: The licensing fees go directly into funding the core development team and maintaining the Open Source version.

docs.title: Commercial FAQ
docs.text:
Q: Can I sell a game built on AeroBeat?

A: Yes. However, if you use the free Community Edition (GPLv3), you must release your game's source code to your players. If you wish to keep your source code private, you must purchase a Commercial License.

Q: Can I release on Consoles (Switch, PS5, Xbox)?

A: Not with the Community Edition. Console SDKs are protected by NDAs that are legally incompatible with the GPLv3 license. You need a Commercial License to legally deploy AeroBeat to consoles.

Q: Can I use the default songs and skins in my commercial game?

A: No. The content in our Art repositories (aerobeat-skins-*, aerobeat-avatars-*, etc.) is licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0 (Non-Commercial). You must replace these assets with your own art and music, or contact us to license specific assets.

Q: Does AeroBeat take a revenue cut?

A: The Open Source version is free. Commercial Licenses are negotiated based on the project scope (Flat fee or Royalty).

Q: I am a hardware vendor. Do I need a commercial license to write a driver?

A: No. Input drivers use MPL 2.0, which allows linking to proprietary SDKs. You can write and distribute a driver for your hardware without paying us or opening your SDK source code.

docs.title: For Creatives
docs.text: Our creative content is protected differently than our code to prevent "Asset Flips."

docs.title: Content Packs (Skins, Avatars, Environments)
docs.text:
• License: CC BY-NC 4.0 (Attribution-NonCommercial)
  • Can I use these assets for fun? Yes! You can remix them, mod them, and share them.
  • Can I use them in a commercial game? No. You cannot take our 3D models, textures, or music and sell them in your own product.
  • Can I claim I made them? No. You must give credit to AeroBeat-Workouts.

docs.title: Documentation (aerobeat-docs)
docs.text:
• License: CC BY-NC 4.0 (Attribution-NonCommercial)
  • The Rule: Our Wiki, GDD, and Architecture docs are community-owned. If you improve them, you must share those improvements under the same license. You cannot use our documentation for commercial means.

docs.title: For Software Engineers
docs.text: Our codebase is split into three categories. The license depends on which repository you are touching.

docs.title: 1. The Shared Core Lanes (aerobeat-input-core, aerobeat-feature-core, aerobeat-content-core, aerobeat-asset-core, aerobeat-ui-core, aerobeat-tool-core)
docs.text:
• License: Mozilla Public License 2.0 (MPL 2.0)
  • The Rule: If you modify these files, you must share your changes.
  • The Nuance: This is "File-Level Copyleft." You can link proprietary modules to these shared cores (for example a closed-source SDK wrapped behind an input or tool integration) as long as the core files themselves remain open.

docs.title: 2. The Game Client (aerobeat-assembly-* & aerobeat-feature-*)
docs.text:
• License: GNU GPLv3
  • The Rule: The "Product" is strictly Open Source. If you distribute a modified version of the AeroBeat Game Client, you must release the entire source code.
  • Why: This prevents corporations from taking our community's work, closing the source, and selling it as a clone.

docs.title: 3. Hardware Drivers (aerobeat-input-*)
docs.text:
• License: Mozilla Public License 2.0 (MPL 2.0)
  • The Rule: Input drivers are treated like the Engine Core.
  • Why: We want hardware vendors (e.g., Smart Watch makers, VR Headset companies) to write official drivers for AeroBeat. MPL 2.0 allows them to link their proprietary SDKs (DLLs/Libs) to our Input System without violating the license.

docs.title: 4. The UI Stack (aerobeat-ui-*)
docs.text:
• UI Core & Kits (aerobeat-ui-core, aerobeat-ui-kit-*): Mozilla Public License 2.0 (MPL 2.0)
    • The Rule: These are treated as standard libraries. You can link them to proprietary shells if needed, but changes to the files themselves must be shared.
  • UI Shells (aerobeat-ui-shell-*): GNU GPLv3
    • The Rule: The Shell contains the specific application flow and wiring. It is treated as part of the Game Client and must be open source.
    • Why: This prevents bad actors from taking the community-built interface, rebranding it as their own, and closing the source.

docs.title: 5. Shared Tools (aerobeat-tool-*)
docs.text:
• License: Mozilla Public License 2.0 (MPL 2.0)
  • The Rule: Treated as libraries.
  • Why: These are generic services (API clients, Analytics) that can be used in proprietary forks without infecting the application logic, provided file-level changes are shared.

docs.title: For Hardware Partners
docs.text:
If you are a hardware vendor looking to integrate your device (Smart Glove, Haptic Vest, XR Headset) with AeroBeat:

  1. You are safe. Our Input Tier (aerobeat-input-*) uses MPL 2.0.
  2. No Viral Infection. You can create a repository aerobeat-input-yourbrand that contains a mix of Open Source GDScript and your Closed Source SDK/DLLs.
  3. Linking is Allowed. As long as you do not modify the AeroBeat Core files themselves, your proprietary logic remains yours.

docs.title: Release Notes
docs.text:
This page is auto-generated from GitHub Releases during deployment.

Run python scripts/generate_changelog.py to update locally.
